summary: Uncle Bob argues that Gherkin's GIVEN/WHEN/THEN syntax has become so effective for specifying requirements because it implicitly describes finite state machine transitions. He draws a parallel between Gherkin (GWT) and unit test patterns (Arrange/Act/Assert), showing they are isomorphic—both describe state transitions. The post demonstrates how viewing requirements as state machines helps identify missing edge cases by systematically exploring all possible state-event pairs. Uncle Bob concludes that Gherkin succeeds because it bridges specification, testing, and programming disciplines with a common language rooted in formal state machine notation.
key_points: Gherkin's GIVEN/WHEN/THEN triplet is fundamentally a state machine transition specification (current state, event, next state)
The Arrange/Act/Assert pattern in unit tests is isomorphic to GIVEN/WHEN/THEN—both describe state transitions
A complete suite of Gherkin scenarios combined with TDD unit tests specifies the complete finite state machine of system behavior
Viewing systems as state machines makes it trivial to find missing state-event pairs that cause bugs
Gherkin succeeds because it speaks equally to specification, testing, and programming disciplines
money_quotes: This means that a suite of Gherkin scenarios is a specification of a finite state machine.
Gherkin is a language that sits perfectly between, and speaks equally to, the three disciplines of specification, testing, and programming.
How often have you found, and fixed, bugs in systems that were due to some event happening in a state that didn't expect it? If you are like me, it happens all the time.
When a system is described in state transition form, and when the states and transitions are well identified, then the hunt for the missing {state, event} pairs becomes trivial.
The formalism that business analysts find most conducive to specifying system requirements just happens to be the same formalism that programmers frequently use to specify system behavior.
There is no better mechanism for exploring the behavior of a system than a well specified state machine.
key_insight: Gherkin's effectiveness stems from its implicit description of finite state machines, which provides a formal bridge between business requirements, tests, and code while making it easy to discover missing edge cases.
